name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, operating through vertically integrated iron ore mining, pellet production, and primary steelmaking with downstream finishing, stamping, tooling, and tubing. The company serves automotive, infrastructure, and manufacturing markets with hot-rolled, cold-rolled, coated, stainless, electrical, and plate products. Transformed through acquisitions of AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, and Stelco. Founded in 1847, headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cleveland-Cliffs executed one of the most transformative acquisition strategies in industrial history — acquiring AK Steel (2020), ArcelorMittal USA (2020), Ferrous Processing & Trading (2021), and Stelco (2024) to build the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America from an iron ore mining base [1]. This represents a genuine business model transformation. However, no organic product breakthroughs or new market creation. AI Deployment (1/2): The company formalized a partnership with Palantir in April 2026, with CEO Goncalves stating 'Palantir's platform allows us to solve problems in ways humans simply cannot' — deploying AI for operational optimization across integrated steelmaking operations [2]. Market Share (1/2): Dominant #1 position in North American flat-rolled steel with 16.2M net tons shipped in 2025 and multi-year fixed-price contracts signed with all major automotive OEMs [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Q1 2026 revenue of $4.92B (up from $4.63B a year ago) with guidance for 16.5-17.0M net tons in 2026 and $10/ton unit cost reductions, plus the slab contract termination expected to deliver ~$700M revenue improvement at current prices [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is modest for a commodity steel producer; the cancelled $500M green steel project signals capital discipline over innovation investment.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cleveland-Cliffs's implicit mission — providing high-quality steel for American manufacturing and domestic supply chain security — is clear and connects to a genuine societal purpose. Goncalves has consistently articulated the vision of rebuilding American steelmaking [2]. However, it reads more as industrial policy advocacy than a transformative purpose statement. Community Impact (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is often the largest employer in its steel towns across Ohio, Indiana, Pennsylvania, and West Virginia. The company publishes sustainability reports with GHG reduction commitments (25% by 2030 from 2017 levels) and community engagement programs [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): No specific retention or tenure data available. Heavy industry faces inherent workforce challenges, and the integration of four major acquisitions in four years creates cultural disruption. Union contracts provide stability but limit organizational flexibility. Insider Buying (1/2): COO Clifford Smith purchased $2.1M in shares in February 2026, and board members Jane Cronin and Ron Bloom purchased $37.5K and $40K respectively in April 2026, demonstrating some insider conviction at depressed share prices [4].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored with growth stock weightings. D/E of 129.3% is above the 70% target but below the 300% floor, reflecting acquisition-driven leverage with $7.8B debt against only $45M cash [1]. ROE is deeply negative at -18.6% scoring 0.0 with 2x weight. Revenue growth of 6.3% YoY is below the 10% target. 3-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% (from $22.99B in 2022 to $18.61B in 2025) is below the -5% floor, scoring 0.0 with 2x weight. Forward P/E of 23.1x is moderately above target, scoring 0.922. Profit margin is -6.4% scoring 0.0. Forward PEG of 0.43 scores maximum (1.0) with 2x weight, reflecting that the deep cyclical recovery pricing implies strong earnings growth relative to valuation. Liquidity of $180.7M daily dollar volume is ample.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Steel manufacturing is fundamentally a physical-world, capital-intensive, energy-intensive industry where AI cannot substitute the core activity of converting iron ore to molten steel via blast furnaces or electric arc furnaces [2]. Cleveland-Cliffs's integrated operations — from mining to smelting to rolling to stamping — require massive physical infrastructure, skilled labor, and deep customer relationships (multi-year fixed-price automotive contracts) that AI cannot replicate. AI actually increases demand for steel through data center construction, infrastructure investment, and manufacturing automation requiring steel components. Analysts do not cite AI as a displacement risk. The only exposure is that AI-optimized supply chains could enable smaller, more nimble competitors to erode distribution advantages. AI Opportunity (1/1): The Palantir partnership formalized in April 2026 deploys AI across integrated steelmaking operations for process optimization, quality control, and predictive maintenance, with Goncalves explicitly citing measurable operational benefits [2].

infinite_game.summary: CEO Goncalves has demonstrated extraordinary courage and existential flexibility in transforming Cleveland-Cliffs from a niche iron ore miner into the dominant North American flat-rolled steel producer through four major acquisitions in four years. However, his combative public persona — attacking US Steel, lobbying aggressively for tariffs, and using confrontational language toward competitors — reflects finite game competitive thinking. The company plays to win market share rather than to advance a cause.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Goncalves's articulated cause — rebuilding American steel manufacturing and ensuring domestic supply chain security — is genuine, service-oriented, and specific. His decade-long advocacy for steel tariffs and domestic procurement reflects consistent strategic conviction [2]. However, the framing is more political and competitive ('make American steel dominant') than visionary, and Sinek would note that the purpose is defined against competitors and foreign imports rather than toward a future state that transcends the industry.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a core leadership team (COO Cliff Smith, CFO Celso Goncalves) through four major acquisitions without catastrophic cultural failures, integrating 25,000+ employees across diverse operations [2]. Union relationships provide structural stability. Board includes experienced directors (Ron Bloom, former Obama steel advisor). However, the CEO's confrontational public style and autocratic decision-making create a top-down culture that limits the psychological safety Sinek values. Heavy industry workforce relations are inherently structured rather than trust-based.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves's public language toward US Steel, Nippon Steel, and foreign producers is deeply combative — focused on blocking acquisitions, winning tariff battles, and 'crushing' import competition through political channels rather than learning from rivals' strengths [2]. His war of words around the US Steel-Nippon Steel deal exemplifies finite competitive thinking. Sinek would see this as the antithesis of the worthy rival concept.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): The transformation from an iron ore miner to North America's largest integrated flat-rolled steel producer through four acquisitions in four years (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, Stelco) is textbook existential flexibility [1]. The company fundamentally reinvented its business model, moving from commodity mining into value-added steel production and downstream processing. This was driven by strategic vision about vertical integration, not desperation.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves's four acquisitions — including ArcelorMittal USA for $1.4B during the COVID pandemic — required extraordinary financial courage [1]. Maintaining capital investment through the 2025 steel downcycle (GAAP net loss of $1.2B) while competitors cut spending demonstrates willingness to invest through adversity. The Palantir AI partnership during a loss year shows forward investment. However, cancelling the $500M green steel project suggests courage has limits when short-term finances are stressed.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is a cyclical turnaround with identifiable 2026 catalysts. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.92B (up 6.3% YoY) showed improvement, and the slab supply contract termination is expected to deliver approximately $700M in revenue improvement at current market prices with ~$150M in conversion cost increase, representing the single largest margin lever [1]. Multi-year fixed-price contracts with all major automotive OEMs lock in high-value volume, and 2026 guidance calls for 16.5-17.0M net tons shipped with $10/ton unit cost reductions. The Palantir AI partnership represents a meaningful operational efficiency initiative. At a forward PEG of 0.43 and $10.70 share price, the stock is priced for continued distress while the catalysts point toward recovery.
thesis_continued: The bear case is formidable: FY2025 produced a GAAP net loss with EPS of -$2.31, ROE of -18.6%, and negative operating cash flow of -$436M, demonstrating that the acquisition-heavy balance sheet ($7.8B debt, only $45M cash) is extremely vulnerable to steel price downturns [1][4]. Revenue has declined from $23.0B (2022) to $18.6B (2025), a 3-year CAGR of -6.8%, driven by steel price weakness. D/E of 129% and minimal cash reserves leave no margin of safety if the recovery stalls. The EV transition creates uncertainty for the ~30% automotive revenue exposure — lighter-weight materials and fewer body-in-white components per EV could structurally reduce steel demand. Chinese overcapacity and global trade tensions add pricing pressure even with tariff protections.
key_catalysts: Slab contract termination delivering ~$700M annual revenue improvement and meaningful EBITDA recovery beginning in 2026, representing the largest single financial improvement lever in the company's history
U.S. steel tariff protections providing pricing floor support, with potential tariff expansion further benefiting domestic producers against Chinese and other foreign overcapacity
Palantir AI partnership driving measurable operational efficiency gains across integrated steelmaking operations, with $10/ton unit cost reduction target for 2026
key_risks: Balance sheet fragility with $7.8B debt against $45M cash and negative free cash flow — any delay in the cyclical recovery or steel price weakness could trigger covenant concerns or require dilutive capital raises

Automotive demand uncertainty as the EV transition shifts OEM production mix — lighter-weight materials, fewer body-in-white steel components per vehicle, and OEM production slowdowns could reduce the ~30% automotive revenue stream

3-year revenue decline from $23.0B to $18.6B (-6.8% CAGR) reflects structural challenges beyond cyclicality — Chinese overcapacity and global trade disruption could prevent full pricing recovery even with domestic tariff protections
sources: Cleveland-Cliffs Q1 2026 and Q4 2025 Earnings Releases — clevelandcliffs.com, February and May 2026
Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Goncalves and Palantir AI Partnership — steelmarketupdate.com, Fortune, April 2026
Yahoo Finance — CLF Key Statistics and Insider Transactions, accessed May 2026
